title: AI hallucination court sanctions tracker
description: US court sanctions and attorney discipline for AI-fabricated citations, quotations, and misstated authority in filings, with docket numbers, legal bases, outcomes, verification levels, and primary sources. Matter-level records, not a comprehensive incident count. Not legal advice; confirm against the linked primary sources.
citation: Vorp Labs, AI Hallucination Court Sanctions Tracker (as of 2026-07-19), https://vorplabs.com/ai-regulatory-updates/ai-hallucination-sanctions

matters.caption: Mata v. Avianca, Inc.
matters.court: US District Court, Southern District of New York
matters.toolNamedInRecord: ChatGPT, established in the sanctions record
matters.fabrication: Six nonexistent cases with fabricated opinions, false quotations, and false propositions; counsel later filed AI-generated copies of the fake opinions.
matters.legalBasis: Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 11; bad-faith analysis
matters.outcome: $5,000 jointly against the attorneys and firm; corrective letters to the client and to each judge falsely identified as an author.
matters.statusDetail: Sanctions imposed 2023-06-22; no appellate modification identified through the last check.
matters.summary: The seminal US sanctions decision on AI-fabricated authority, cited repeatedly by later courts.

factChecks.label: Docket
factChecks.value: The CourtListener docket for Mata v. Avianca records the show-cause proceedings and the June 2023 sanctions opinion.

matters.caption: Kruse v. Karlen, 692 S.W.3d 43
matters.court: Missouri Court of Appeals, Eastern District
matters.toolNamedInRecord: Unspecified AI used by an online consultant, according to the appellant's reply brief
matters.fabrication: Twenty-two inaccurate case citations, including fictitious cases and real case names attached to citations that did not support the asserted propositions.
matters.outcome: Motion to strike granted as to dismissal and further relief; appeal dismissed; appellant ordered to pay $10,000 in Rule 84.19 damages toward appellate attorney fees.
matters.statusDetail: Published disposition filed 2024-02-13; no later modification identified.
matters.summary: Published appellate treatment of a self-represented litigant who submitted AI-fabricated authorities through an online consultant.

factChecks.label: Opinion
factChecks.value: The published Missouri Court of Appeals opinion in ED111172 was inspected in full, including the pro se posture, twenty-two inaccurate citations, dismissal, and $10,000 damages award.

matters.caption: Concord Music Group, Inc. v. Anthropic PBC
matters.court: US District Court, Northern District of California
matters.toolNamedInRecord: Claude, expressly identified in the order
matters.fabrication: At least one nonexistent article citation and additional inaccurate bibliographic material after the model was used to format citations in an expert filing.
matters.legalBasis: The court's authority to evaluate the expert declaration; lead-counsel accuracy certification required by Judge Lee's civil standing order
matters.outcome: Paragraph 9 of Ms. Chen's declaration struck; the court recorded that the hallucinated citation undermined the overall credibility of her written declaration. No monetary sanction was imposed in this order.
matters.statusDetail: Disposition in Docket 377: paragraph 9 struck and credibility impact recorded; no monetary sanction imposed in that order.
matters.summary: AI-company litigation in which its model hallucinated bibliographic material while formatting an expert's citations, and the court struck the affected paragraph.

factChecks.label: Order
factChecks.value: The May 23, 2025 order (Docket 377) was inspected in full; it strikes paragraph 9 of Ms. Chen's declaration and records the credibility consequence.

openItems: The inspected document is hosted on a researcher's mirror rather than an official court or RECAP URL; the docket entry itself is queued for a direct pull.
Any later monetary or disciplinary consequence after Docket 377 has not been verified.

matters.caption: Stephen McCarthy, P.A. v. United States Drug Enforcement Administration
matters.court: US Court of Appeals for the Third Circuit
matters.fabrication: At least one nonexistent authority and materially inaccurate case summaries in a brief prepared with client-supplied material and AI assistance; counsel minimized rather than promptly corrected the errors.
matters.legalBasis: Pennsylvania Rule of Professional Conduct 1.1; Third Circuit Disciplinary Rule 2.1(d)
matters.outcome: Petitioner's counsel, Daniel A. Pallen, publicly reprimanded; clerk directed to notify other admitting courts and the National Discipline Data Bank. No monetary sanction.
matters.summary: Precedential appellate discipline of attorney Daniel A. Pallen on competence, delegation, and the duty to correct promptly.

factChecks.label: Opinion
factChecks.value: The published Third Circuit opinion was inspected in full; its official caption names Stephen McCarthy, P.A. as petitioner and identifies Daniel A. Pallen as the disciplined attorney.

matters.caption: Fletcher v. Experian Information Solutions, Inc.
matters.court: US Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit
matters.toolNamedInRecord: Generative AI admitted; no product named in the opinion
matters.fabrication: Sixteen fabricated quotations and five additional serious misrepresentations of law or fact in a reply brief.
matters.legalBasis: Federal Rule of Appellate Procedure 46(c) and inherent authority; Rule 11 principles discussed
matters.outcome: $2,500 payable to the court. Evasive show-cause responses were treated as aggravating.
matters.statusDetail: Published sanction imposed 2026-02-18; no later modification identified.
matters.summary: Published circuit decision holding that prompt admission mitigates while concealment and evasion aggravate.

factChecks.label: Opinion
factChecks.value: The published Fifth Circuit opinion imposing the $2,500 sanction was inspected in full on the court's site.

matters.caption: Ibach and Stewart v. Bruce
matters.toolNamedInRecord: Unspecified AI, admitted at the show-cause hearing
matters.fabrication: Widespread nonexistent cases, false quotations, and materially misstated holdings; the court found most authorities in the briefs were misquoted or nonexistent.
matters.legalBasis: Alabama Rules of Appellate Procedure 2(a)(2), 28(a)(10), 38, and 25A, with the court's appellate and disciplinary authority
matters.outcome: Counsel delivered a $17,200 check before the opinion; the April 24 opinion then ordered $17,200 in attorney fees and costs, crediting any amount already paid. It also ordered double appellate costs, required another lawyer's signature on future filings, referred counsel to the Alabama State Bar, granted withdrawal, and dismissed the appeal as a sanction.
matters.statusDetail: Sanctions imposed 2026-04-24; state bar outcome not verified as of the last check.
matters.summary: State supreme court treatment with dismissal of the client's appeal and an explicit proportionality discussion.

factChecks.label: Opinion
factChecks.value: The April 24, 2026 Alabama Supreme Court opinion in SC-2025-0106 was inspected in full, including the order for $17,200 in attorney fees and costs with credit for amounts already paid.

openItems: State appellate portal URLs are tokenized and can rot; the document is also identified by caption and docket for re-retrieval.

matters.caption: Lnu v. Blanche
matters.court: US Court of Appeals for the Ninth Circuit
matters.toolNamedInRecord: Unspecified generative AI; inferred by the court, later admitted
matters.fabrication: Two nonexistent cases, two quotations falsely attributed to real cases, gross misrepresentations of other authorities, and attempts to characterize the errors as typographical.
matters.legalBasis: Federal Rule of Appellate Procedure 46(b); Ninth Circuit Rule 46-2; FRAP 28; California Rules of Professional Conduct 1.1, 1.3, 3.1, and 3.3
matters.outcome: Each attorney fined $2,500 and suspended from Ninth Circuit practice for six months; notice to clients, opposing counsel, judges, and firm attorneys; a two-year firm-wide AI-use and citation-verification certification requirement; referral to licensing authorities.
matters.statusDetail: Circuit discipline imposed 2026-06-03; state bar proceedings pending or unverified as of the last check.
matters.summary: The most comprehensive published federal appellate remedial package located: money, suspension, disclosure, firm-wide controls, and bar referral.

factChecks.label: Order
factChecks.value: The June 3, 2026 Ninth Circuit disciplinary order in No. 24-4790 was inspected in full on the court's site.

corroboratedMatters.caption: Park v. Kim, 91 F.4th 610
corroboratedMatters.court: US Court of Appeals for the Second Circuit
corroboratedMatters.reportedOutcome: Referral to the circuit's grievance panel over nonexistent authority generated with ChatGPT; no monetary sanction in the published decision. The decision rejected the argument that lawyers needed an AI-specific warning before being held responsible for false filings.
corroboratedMatters.corroboration: Discussed in the Fifth Circuit's published Fletcher opinion, which was inspected in full.

corroboratedMatters.caption: Smith v. Farwell, No. 2282CV01197
corroboratedMatters.reportedOutcome: $2,000 court sanction over fictitious citations, followed later by a public reprimand from the Massachusetts Board of Bar Overseers; one of the earliest matters to proceed from court sanction to public bar discipline.
corroboratedMatters.corroboration: The public reprimand is confirmed by an official Illinois judiciary article on AI hallucination fallout, which was fetched directly.

corroborationSource.label: Illinois courts article confirming the reprimand

corroboratedMatters.caption: Johnson v. Dunn, No. 2:21-cv-01701
corroboratedMatters.court: US District Court, Northern District of Alabama
corroboratedMatters.reportedOutcome: Sanctions imposed on institutional defense counsel over false or nonexistent cases in two filings; frequently cited by later courts compiling hallucination authority. The exact amount is not asserted here pending the primary order.
corroboratedMatters.corroboration: Expressly confirmed in the Alabama Supreme Court's Ibach opinion, which was inspected in full.

corroborationSource.label: Ibach opinion citing Johnson (Alabama portal PDF)

caveats: The unit is a legal decision or document, not a unique underlying matter; one lawsuit can produce several rows.
The database backfills older decisions, so growth between snapshots is not a clean incident-rate series.
Inclusion generally requires a court to notice and address the problem, so it undercounts actual bad filings.
It records warnings and no-sanction dispositions as well as sanctions; it is not a sanctions-only count.

adjacentMaterials.title: Order on Artificial Intelligence
adjacentMaterials.summary: Early court-level order requiring disclosure concerning generative-AI-drafted submissions and addressing confidentiality.

adjacentMaterials.issuer: US District Court, District of Kansas
adjacentMaterials.title: Standing Order 26-01 on the use of AI
adjacentMaterials.summary: District-wide standing order addressing AI-generated fabricated or incorrect authority and available sanctions.

adjacentMaterials.issuer: US Court of Appeals for the Fifth Circuit
adjacentMaterials.title: Proposed Local Rule 32.3 on AI certification, declined
adjacentMaterials.summary: A proposed certification rule for generative-AI use was published for comment and not adopted; the court concluded existing rules were sufficient. The history is set out in the published Fletcher opinion.

sources.label: Fletcher opinion recounting the rule history (court PDF)

adjacentMaterials.title: Formal Opinion 512: Generative Artificial Intelligence Tools
adjacentMaterials.summary: Lawyers remain accountable for competence, confidentiality, supervision, meritorious claims, candor, and fees; AI-generated analysis and citations must be reviewed before submission and errors corrected. americanbar.org blocks automated clients; the opinion PDF was verified via an archived snapshot.

adjacentMaterials.title: AI-specific comments to Rule of Professional Conduct 1.1
adjacentMaterials.summary: Colorado materials describe amended Rule 1.1 comments effective January 2026 as the first US jurisdiction-specific AI amendments to lawyer ethics rules.

adjacentMaterials.title: ARDC guidance on implementing AI, with sanctions fallout review
adjacentMaterials.summary: Official Illinois judiciary article connecting hallucinated citations to court sanctions and bar discipline, linking the ARDC implementation guide.

changeLog.label: Verification correction
changeLog.change: Corrected Concord's paragraph-strike disposition, Ibach's ordered attorney-fee award and release date, Fletcher's filing date, and the McCarthy caption and disciplined-attorney identity. Kruse moved from corroborated to primary-verified after inspection of the published opinion.

changeLog.label: Tracker
changeLog.change: Tracker launched with six primary-verified matters, four corroborated matters, adjacent rules and guidance, and the landscape snapshot. A July 2026 lead queue from the independent databases is in verification and enters the registry only after the order documents are pulled.

changeLog.label: Ninth Circuit, Lnu v. Blanche
changeLog.change: Disciplinary order imposed $2,500 fines, six-month practice suspensions, notice duties, a two-year firm-wide AI certification requirement, and licensing referrals.

changeLog.label: Third Circuit, McCarthy v. DEA
changeLog.change: Precedential public reprimand for AI-assisted briefing errors, with notice to other admitting courts and the National Discipline Data Bank.
